content:
{"novelty":4,"feasibility":8,"effectiveness":3,"overall":4}

This idea applies existing self-reflection and multi-turn prompting techniques to adversarial robustness. The core mechanism—having models self-assess and critique outputs—has been explored in constitutional AI, self-refinement, and various prompt-based safety methods. The "chain" framing borrows heavily from chain-of-thought without substantial innovation. While combining these for defense is reasonable, it's incremental rather than novel.

Feasibility is high since it only requires API access and existing datasets. Implementation is straightforward—no training, no special infrastructure.

Effectiveness is questionable. A fundamental limitation: if the model is already jailbroken or manipulated by the adversarial prompt, asking it to self-assess using the same compromised reasoning is unlikely to help. Adversarial prompts often work precisely because they manipulate the model's internal reasoning and safety checks. Recursive self-assessment won't fix this—the model will rationalize harmful outputs as acceptable. Existing work on self-critique shows models are poor at catching their own errors without external grounding. Strong adaptive attacks will likely bypass this easily by targeting the constraint-check prompts themselves.

The approach also risks significant overhead (multiple API calls per query) and may reduce helpfulness by over-constraining benign requests. Without model internals access or training, prompt-only defenses face inherent limitations against sophisticated attacks.
